channel.title: NYT > Science
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: For NASA’s Artemis II Crew, Journey to the Moon ‘Starting to Feel Real’
item.description: The four astronauts — three Americans and one Canadian — spoke from a prelaunch quarantine ahead of their scheduled Wednesday mission.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:description: From left, Jeremy Hansen, Christina Koch, Reid Wiseman and Victor Glover, members of the Artemis II crew, after arriving at the Kennedy Space Center in Cape Canaveral, Fla., on Friday. Mr. Wiseman is holding a plush toy named Rise that will fly in their capsule during their journey to the moon.

item.title: The Fragile Hope for Salmon Recovery in Maine
item.description: A long-term project to remove or modify dams may clear the way for endangered wild Atlantic salmon to swim freely up to the Sandy River. But it faces opposition from business and lawmakers.
item.dc:creator: Murray Carpenter and Greta Rybus
item.media:credit: Greta Rybus for The New York Times

item.title: Did Scientists Just Detect an Exploding Black Hole?
item.description: An underwater observatory recently detected a startlingly energetic cosmic neutrino. One possible cause involves a phenomenon that so far exists only in theory.
item.dc:creator: Dennis Overbye

item.title: Glass Threads Spun From a Volcano’s Bubbly Magma
item.description: A volcanic formation known as Pele’s hair is produced when air-filled magma is stretched, according to a new study by a team of scientists and glass artists.
item.dc:creator: Katherine Kornei
item.media:description: A handful of Pele’s hair, extremely thin strands of volcanic glass are named for Pele, a female figure in Hawaiian mythology associated with volcanism, after an eruption of Kilauea volcano in Hawaii last March.

item.title: How to Turn a Chicken Egg Into a Drug Factory
item.description: A biotech start-up is testing a novel way of efficiently producing pharmaceutical drugs.
item.dc:creator: Carl Zimmer

item.title: Humans Had Dogs Before They Had Farming, Ancient DNA Confirms
item.description: New research pushes the first genetic evidence of dogs back by 5,000 years and suggests that hunter-gatherer groups may have acquired dogs from one another.
item.dc:creator: Emily Anthes

item.title: Ancient Grapes Reveal Long History of Modern Wines
item.description: Genetic material preserved in ancient grape seeds reveals when, and how, humans meddled with wine grapes.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Dzombak
item.media:description: Humans in France were domesticating grapes for making wine by 650 B.C. or earlier, around the time when Greek settlers founded the port city of Marseilles, according to a new study.

item.title: We’ve Been Underestimating Flying Foxes
item.description: Australia used to incinerate the large fruit bats, but research suggests they bring hundreds of millions of dollars in benefits to the country’s economy.
item.dc:creator: Anthony Ham

item.title: Wicked Stepmother No Longer, a Female Pharoah Gets a Reputational Makeover
item.description: A reassessment of damaged 3,500-year-old statuary adds to evidence that Queen Hatshepsut wasn’t the villain that scholars long took her to be.
item.dc:creator: Franz Lidz

item.title: Australian Sky Turns an Apocalyptic Blood Red
item.description: Winds from Tropical Cyclone Narelle stirred up rust-colored dust from iron-rich soil, tinging the sky over Western Australia with a reddish Mars-like hue.
item.dc:creator: Johnny Diaz

item.title: A Secret History of Psychosis
item.description: Cohen Miles-Rath heard voices telling him to kill his father. After they passed, he spent years retracing the path of his delusions.
item.dc:creator: Ellen Barry
item.media:credit: Celia Talbot Tobin for The New York Times

item.title: Astronaut’s Condition That Led to Space Station Evacuation Remains a Mystery
item.description: The astronaut, Michael Fincke, experienced a medical emergency in January that rendered him unable to speak, he said.
item.dc:creator: Rylee Kirk
item.media:description: Mike Fincke, a NASA astronaut, said doctors aren’t sure what caused his medical episode, which prompted an evacuation from the International Space Station.

item.title: Henry C. Lee Dies at 87; Forensic Scientist Testified in Defense of O.J. Simpson
item.description: The Times called him “the world’s most highly regarded forensic criminologist,” but later in his career he faced accusations that he had hidden and fabricated evidence.
item.dc:creator: Jeré Longman
item.media:description: Henry C. Lee in 2011 at the University of New Haven in Connecticut, where he was a professor for more than 50 years. He had many high-profile cases, but none drew more attention than the 1995 murder trial of O.J. Simpson.

item.title: Dr. Judith L. Rapoport, Pioneer in Understanding O.C.D., Dies at 92
item.description: Her best-selling 1989 book, “The Boy Who Couldn’t Stop Washing,” based on her groundbreaking research, brought public awareness to obsessive-compulsive disorder.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel
item.category: Boy Who Couldn't Stop Washing, The (Book)

item.title: Clean Energy Companies Are Trying to Survive the Trump Era
item.description: Offshore wind is out. Geothermal power is in. And many climate technology start-ups are looking for ways to carry on without federal backing.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.media:description: A tour of the Geothermal House of the CERAWeek energy conference in Houston this week. Geothermal energy is emerging as one of the big green winners of the current era.

item.title: He Helped Write the Clean Air Act. He Fears for Its Future.
item.description: Thomas Jorling, adviser to Republicans who cosponsored the 1970 law, disputes the Trump administration’s claim that it shouldn’t apply to planet-warming greenhouse gases.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Cindy Schultz for The New York Times

item.title: Robert Trivers, Eccentric Scientist Who Probed Human Nature, Dies at 83
item.description: A visionary evolutionary biologist, he drew comparisons to Charles Darwin with his theories on the genetic roots of seemingly detrimental behaviors like self-deception.
item.dc:creator: Michael S. Rosenwald

item.title: Japan and the U.S. Agree to Team Up on Seabed Mining
item.description: The arrangement could signal a fracture in the decades-long effort among nations to reach consensus on how to mine the ocean floor while protecting ecosystems.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey
item.media:description: A Japanese seabed mining research vessel in Shimizu, Japan, in January.

item.title: Jesse Roth, Who Advanced the Understanding of Diabetes, Dies at 91
item.description: His research showed that Type 2 diabetes was caused by insulin resistance at the cellular level, a controversial idea that initially met with disbelief.
item.dc:creator: Delthia Ricks
item.category: National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases

item.title: As Kennedy Takes on Food Policy, Companies Push Back
item.description: Though the health secretary falsely claimed a victory in a recent podcast, a federal proposal to review all new food ingredients is still in the early stages and drawing fierce opposition.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett
item.media:description: Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. at a public unveiling of new dietary guidelines at the Department of Health and Human Services headquarters in Washington last month.

item.title: An Invisible Bottleneck: A Helium Shortage Threatens the Chip Industry
item.description: With a third of the global supply offline because of the war in Iran, gas companies are scrambling to assure critical A.I. chip makers there will be no disruptions.
item.dc:creator: Meaghan Tobin and Xinyun Wu
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Lam Yik Fei for The New York Times
item.media:description: Air Liquide supplies helium and other gases to the semiconductor industry.

item.title: Winter Sea Ice in the Arctic Ties a Record Low
item.description: Ice plays a vital role in reflecting away planet-warming sunlight. The Arctic is warming much faster than most other parts of the world.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey and Harry Stevens
item.category: National Snow and Ice Data Center
item.media:description: The disappearing ice also affects wildlife habitat. A polar bear on Norwegian sea ice last April.

item.title: Scientists Filmed a Whale Birth. The Surprise: Mom Had Many Helpers.
item.description: The episode, involving a group of sperm whales, adds to evidence that humans aren’t the only species that gets some form of assistance during and after delivery.
item.dc:creator: Catrin Einhorn

item.title: Wealthy Investors Target Foes of Clean Energy, Seeking Revenge
item.description: Renewable energy leaders said their industry got “rolled” in President Trump’s tax bill. Now they’re fighting back, starting in Texas.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman and Brad Plumer
item.media:credit: Mason Trinca for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Roseland solar and storage facility in Riesel, Texas. The state ranks first in wind and second in solar capacity in the country, generating nearly $300 million in state and local taxes annually.

item.title: Schumer Pledges Democrats Will Restore Clean Energy Tax Credits
item.description: Senator Chuck Schumer of New York, the minority leader, said Democrats would pursue an agenda to reduce energy costs if they win back control of Congress.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Chuck Schumer, the Senate minority leader, at the Capitol this week.

item.title: Trump to Delay Nominating New C.D.C. Director
item.description: The administration has yet to find a candidate who aligns with Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr.’s agenda while avoiding his unpopular stance on vaccines.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli and Sheryl Gay Stolberg
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: Under federal law, the White House had until midnight on Wednesday to nominate a replacement for former Centers for Disease Control and Prevention director Susan Monarez.

item.title: Heisuke Hironaka, Groundbreaking Mathematician, Is Dead at 94
item.description: A recipient of his profession’s prestigious Fields Medal, he devised an algorithm that helps solve mathematical “singularities.” It now permeates the field.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:credit: The Asahi Shimbun, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Heisuke Hironaka in 1975, during a period when he was giving lectures and appearing on Japanese television.

item.title: E.P.A. Waives Smog Rules on Summer Gasoline in Bid to Ease Prices
item.description: The moves will allow expanded sales of a higher-ethanol blend known as E15, which is often restricted in the summer.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer and Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: An ethanol plant in Rochelle, Ill. The gasoline blend E15 — which consists of 15 percent ethanol — is restricted in the summer because it evaporates more easily in the heat, leading to smog and decreased efficiency.

item.title: Maryland Supreme Court Strikes Down Local Climate Suit Against Big Oil
item.description: The decision represents a setback to other local governments around the country that have sued oil companies to recoup the mounting costs of climate change.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:description: Crude oil storage in Baltimore. The governments of Baltimore, Annapolis and Anne Arundel County had sued 26 oil and gas companies to recover damages caused by greenhouse gas emissions.

item.title: NASA Sets Out New Plans and Timelines for Moon Base and Nuclear Mars Mission
item.description: The agency’s leader said new plans and timelines for the coming decade aim to create a permanent foothold by humans on another world and inspire Americans.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:description: NASA’s Artemis II rocket system at sunrise on Tuesday. Among other objectives, the agency announced plans to speed the pace of Artemis missions, from one every few years to twice a year.

item.title: How Do You Measure Snow From Space? First, Climb a Mountain.
item.description: A new satellite could transform how water is studied worldwide. But to help unlock its capabilities, scientists first needed to take critical measurements on a mountaintop.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey and Nina Riggio
item.media:credit: Nina Riggio for The New York Times

item.title: Tango Therapy: How the Dance of Passion Is Helping Parkinson’s Patients
item.description: Once a week, patients in an Argentine hospital with Parkinson’s disease use the movements of tango to help address issues of balance, stiffness and coordination.
item.dc:creator: Magalí Druscovich and Pam Belluck

item.title: MDMA Therapy in Australia Shows Results for PTSD Patients, but the Cost Is Limiting Access
item.description: The country’s experiment with psychedelic medicine has led to positive outcomes, psychiatrists say, but also highlights the limitations of the nascent field.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Jacobs
item.media:credit: Matthew Abbott for The New York Times
item.media:description: With the blinds drawn to reduce natural light, Zoe Marshall takes a quiet moment as clinicians begin the dosing process for her MDMA-assisted therapy session in Sydney, Australia.

item.title: Carbon Capture Technology Is Helping This Pub Make Beer
item.description: A pub in California is pulling carbon dioxide from the air to carbonate pints. If the business model works, it could give the broader carbon-capture industry a boost.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Nuwer and Ian C. Bates
item.media:credit: Ian C. Bates for The New York Times

item.title: Takeaways From The Times’s Inside Look at the C.D.C.
item.description: Many current and former employees say the actions of Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. are undermining the agency’s role in safeguarding public health.
item.dc:creator: Jeneen Interlandi
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

item.title: Diabetes, Overlooked and Unchecked, Poses New Risks in Africa
item.description: As deaths from diabetes start to rival those from infectious threats like malaria, a new form of the condition linked to malnutrition is surfacing in patients who can afford neither screening nor care.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Nolen and Arlette Bashizi
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Habiba Sali, right, a nurse in the endocrinology department of the Maroua Regional Hospital in the far north region of Cameroon, took the vital signs of patient Aminatou Mana during a regular checkup.
